content:
Go scaled our team for seven years. It stopped scaling our tail.

9 min read
Just now

--

The first thing that told us something was wrong wasn’t a latency graph. It was the finance channel.
Our infra spend for the dispatch tier had grown 3.1x in eighteen months while request volume had grown 1.9x. Nobody had shipped a regression. No incident had been filed. The autoscaler was doing exactly what it had been told to do, and what it had been told to do was keep adding pods until p99 came back under the SLO. It was adding a lot of pods.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

We were at 240 replicas of a service that, on paper, should have run comfortably on sixty.
The Capacity Curve Bent the Wrong Way
Dispatch is our fan-out service. Inbound events land on it, get enriched against an in-memory index, and get written to per-tenant queues. It was one of the first services we broke out of the monolith. Written in Go in 2018, and for most of its life it was the least interesting thing we operated — it did one job, it did it at 20k rps, and it never paged anyone.
The capacity math had always been linear. Double the traffic, double the pods. That relationship quietly stopped holding somewhere around…
